title: From Control To Surrender
study: A Study In The Life Of King David
scriptureForWeek: This week covers a variety of passages on David’s struggles as a father and the consequences of his actions. Make sure you follow the daily scripture locations closely.
weekTheme: Boys neglect, Men focus God will help our families Did you know that the family you grow up in will impact you more than almost anything else in your life? We don’t tend to think about that when we grow up, but it’s true. Our families teach us what our values are. Our families teach us what is right and wrong. Our families give us our sense of identity and worth. Our families can help us, prepare us, and send us out with love. Or our families can wound us, damage us, and send us into the world limping along baggage. King David was actually an amazing leader. In spite of his failure with Bathsheba we looked at last week, he got so much right. He defeated the enemies of Israel, was patient in the process of becoming king, brought the Ark of the Covenant into Jerusalem, and set up an amazing 24/7 worship service that lasted for 30 years. He was a just and fair ruler who brought prosperity and safety to the people of God. But David was busy, a little too busy, and there was one area of his life that was a glaring oversight. David was a bad dad. He spent so much time leading the nation that he forget to lead his home. David didn’t make time for his sons. And the result was a total disaster. His sons rebelled against him, tried to steal his kingdom, were sexually promiscuous, and had no restraint. So much of David’s legacy was destroyed because he forgot to focus on his kids. You may think that being a Dad is so far off for you that it doesn’t matter. But trust me, it's so important to have an idea of how to raise up other men. In the next few years, you are going to start looking around at other kids who aren’t getting what you are getting here on the Primal Path and seeing the damage that comes with an absent or angry

days.title: Disaster Without Discipline
days.scripture: 2 SAMUEL 13 Now David’s son Absalom had a beautiful sister named Tamar. And Amnon, her half brother, fell desperately in love with her. Amnon became so obsessed with Tamar that he became ill. She was a virgin, and Amnon thought he could never have her. But Amnon had a very crafty friend—his cousin Jonadab. He was the son of David’s brother Shimea. One day Jonadab said to Amnon, “What’s the trouble? Why should the son of a king look so dejected morning after morning?” So Amnon told him, “I am in love with Tamar, my brother Absalom’s sister.” “Well,” Jonadab said, “I’ll tell you what to do. Go back to bed and pretend you are ill. When your father comes to see you, ask him to let Tamar come and prepare some food for you. Tell him you’ll feel better if she prepares it as you watch and feeds you with her own hands.” So Amnon lay down and pretended to be sick. And when the king came to see him, Amnon asked him, “Please let my sister Tamar come and cook my favorite dish as I watch. Then I can eat it from her own hands.” So David agreed and sent Tamar to Amnon’s house to prepare some food for him. When Tamar arrived at Amnon’s house, she went to the place where he was lying down so he could watch her mix some dough. Then she baked his favorite dish for him. But when she set the serving tray before him, he refused to eat. “Everyone get out of here,” Amnon told his servants. So they all left. Then he said to Tamar, “Now bring the food into my bedroom and feed it to me here.” So Tamar took his favorite dish to him. But as she was feeding him, he grabbed her and demanded, “Come to bed with me, my darling sister.” “No, my brother!” she cried. “Don’t be foolish! Don’t do this to me! Such wicked things aren’t done in Israel. Where could I go in my shame? And you would be called one of the greatest fools in Israel. Please, just speak to the king about it, and he will let you marry me.” But Amnon wouldn’t listen to her, and since he was stronger than she was, he raped her. Then suddenly Amnon’s love turned to hate, and he hated her even more than he had loved her. “Get out of here!” he snarled at her. “No, no!” Tamar cried. “Sending me away now is worse than what you’ve already done to me.” But Amnon wouldn’t listen to her. He shouted for his servant and demanded, “Throw this woman out, and lock the door behind her!” So the servant put her out and locked the door behind her. She was wearing a long, beautiful robe, as was the custom in those days for the king’s virgin daughters. But now Tamar tore her robe and put ashes on her head. And then, with her face in her hands, she went away crying. Her brother Absalom saw her and asked, “Is it true that Amnon has been with you? Well, my sister, keep quiet for now, since he’s your brother. Don’t you worry about it.” So Tamar lived as a desolate woman in her brother Absalom’s house. When King David heard what had happened, he was very angry. And though Absalom never spoke to Amnon about this, he hated Amnon deeply because of what he had done to his sister. Two years later, when Absalom’s sheep were being sheared at Baal-hazor near Ephraim, Absalom invited all the king’s sons to come to a feast. 24 He went to the king and said, “My sheep-shearers are now at work. Would the king and his servants please come to celebrate the occasion with me?” The king replied, “No, my son. If we all came, we would be too much of a burden on you.” Absalom pressed him, but the king would not come, though he gave Absalom his blessing. “Well, then,” Absalom said, “if you can’t come, how about sending my brother Amnon with us?” “Why Amnon?” the king asked. But Absalom kept on pressing the king until he finally agreed to let all his sons attend, including Amnon. So Absalom prepared a feast fit for a king. Absalom told his men, “Wait until Amnon gets drunk; then at my signal, kill him! Don’t be afraid. I’m the one who has given the command. Take courage and do it!” So at Absalom’s signal they murdered Amnon. Then the other sons of the king jumped on their mules and fled. As they were on the way back to Jerusalem, this report reached David: “Absalom has killed all the king’s sons; not one is left alive!” The king got up, tore his robe, and threw himself on the ground. His advisers also tore their clothes in horror and sorrow. But just then Jonadab, the son of David’s brother Shimea, arrived and said, “No, don’t believe that all the king’s sons have been killed! It was only Amnon! Absalom has been plotting this ever since Amnon raped his sister Tamar. No, my lord the king, your sons aren’t all dead! It was only Amnon.” Meanwhile Absalom escaped. Then the watchman on the Jerusalem wall saw a great crowd coming down the hill on the road from the west. He ran to tell the king, “I see a crowd of people coming from the Horonaim road along the side of the hill.” “Look!” Jonadab told the king. “There they are now! The king’s sons are coming, just as I said.” They soon arrived, weeping and sobbing, and the king and all his servants wept bitterly with them. And David mourned many days for his son Amnon. Absalom fled to his grandfather, Talmai son of Ammihud, the king of Geshur. He stayed there in Geshur for three years. And King David, now reconciled to Amnon’s death, longed to be reunited with his son Absalom.
days.reflection: The chapter you just read feels more like a Hollywood revenge movie than a scene from the bible. Rape, revenge, murder, and mourning. What a tragic story in the middle of David’s kingdom. But it’s even worse. This isn’t happening out on the battlefield or among the soldiers. This is happening with David’s sons. David has failed to discipline his sons and it has led to disaster. One of David’s sons sexually assaulted his half-sister, and David doesn’t do anything about it. Sure, he got angry but being angry wasn’t enough. He needed to step in and make sure that justice was done. But because he refused to act, her brother Absolom felt like he had to take things into his own hands. He schemed and plotted to get revenge on Amnon. He murdered his brother to honor his sister, and then he had to flee. David’s house was filled with absolute chaos because of his failure to act. Imagine this was happening in your family. It's also interesting to note how lust works in this passage. Amnon’s lust dehumanized his sister. It turned her from a person into an object so that a man could experience satisfaction. But as soon as he was satisfied, he disposed of her like a used toy. Godless desires damage people and destroy those involved. Amnon had no idea that his desire would lead to his death. But pursuing what God has forbidden always does in some way or another. So much of what is wrong in the world today is the result of men who were not disciplined as kids. Their parents let them do whatever they wanted, and they never got a moral compass. If a man isn’t taught to control himself, he is going to have to be controlled by power. The power of the police, the power of the law, the power of prison. No one really likes being disciplined when growing up. We want to be able to do what we want, when we want, and how we want. But this will ultimately lead to our own destruction. We are all born with a sinful nature and given the right time and circumstances can do some really terrible things. And you have an enemy who hates you and wants to destroy your life with temptation and regret. Discipline is a gift. If you want to be a wise and mature man, you will have to learn to see it that way. That’s why Proverbs 13:24 says, “Those who spare the rod of discipline hate their children. Those who love their children care enough to discipline them.” If you had a new puppy and you really loved him, you would train him. You wouldn’t let him run out onto the road to get hit by a car. You wouldn’t let him eat whatever he wanted to get sick. You wouldn’t let him run away to do whatever his doggie heart wanted to do. You would have to discipline him if you loved him. You are way more important than a puppy. Your parents want you to thrive. They want you to stay out of danger and the stuff that will really screw you up. And God does too. Learn from David. Surrender your desires to God. Let him discipline them and discipline you. It will lead to life in the end and free you from disaster.
questions: Can you think of a time when you were younger when you were disciplined and hated it, yet can see now that it was actually for your good? What happened, and how do you see it differently now?
Can you think of 3 words that guys your age would think about the word discipline? Why did you choose those words?

Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time when they were your age when they got in trouble and were disciplined. How do they see those events now, and how do they think about it? What advice would they have for you in thinking about embracing discipline?

thoughts.verse: As you endure this divine discipline, remember that God is treating you as his own children. Who ever heard of a child who is never disciplined by its father? If God doesn’t discipline you as he does all of his children, it means that you are illegitimate and are not really his children at all. Since we respected our earthly fathers who disciplined us, shouldn’t we submit even more to the discipline of the Father of our spirits, and live forever? For our earthly fathers disciplined us for a few years, doing the best they knew how. But God’s discipline is always good for us, so that we might share in his holiness. No discipline is enjoyable while it is happening— it’s painful! But afterward there will be a peaceful harvest of right living for those who are trained in this way.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God for your family. Thank him that you have people willing to discipline you for the things you do, so that you learn to be a godly and noble man.

days.title: The Failure Of Favoritism
days.scripture: 2 SAMUEL 18:5 AND 18:33 - 19:8 (BUT YOU CAN READ BOTH CHAPTERS FOR CONTEXT) And the king gave this command to Joab, Abishai, and Ittai: “For my sake, deal gently with young Absalom.” And all the troops heard the king give this order to his commanders. ABSALOM IS KILLED BY DAVID’S MEN, THIS IS DAVID’S RESPONSE. The king was overcome with emotion. He went up to the room over the gateway and burst into tears. And as he went, he cried, “O my son Absalom! My son, my son Absalom! If only I had died instead of you! O Absalom, my son, my son.” Word soon reached Joab that the king was weeping and mourning for Absalom. As all the people heard of the king’s deep grief for his son, the joy of that day’s victory was turned into deep sadness. They crept back into the town that day as though they were ashamed and had deserted in battle. The king covered his face with his hands and kept on crying, “O my son Absalom! O Absalom, my son, my son!” Then Joab went to the king’s room and said to him, “We saved your life today and the lives of your sons, your daughters, and your wives and concubines. Yet you act like this, making us feel ashamed of ourselves. You seem to love those who hate you and hate those who love you. You have made it clear today that your commanders and troops mean nothing to you. It seems that if Absalom had lived and all of us had died, you would be pleased. Now go out there and congratulate your troops, for I swear by the LORD that if you don’t go out, not a single one of them will remain here tonight. Then you will be worse off than ever before.” So the king went out and took his seat at the town gate, and as the news spread throughout the town that he was there, everyone went to him.
days.reflection: We all have favorite things. You probably have a favorite sports team. You probably have a favorite food. You probably have a favorite color. Favorites are ways of showing our preferences and distinctiveness as people. That’s natural and even helpful to get a sense of who you are and what you like. But favoritism is something else. Favoritism is unhealthy. Favoritism means we treat some people better than others. We act unfairly and unjustly and give some people privileges over others. Can you remember back to shift 1, From Ease to Difficulty? Joseph's life was thrown into chaos because his dad treated him as a favorite. It created jealousy and anger and made Joseph proud when he was a teenager. It seems that King David has a favorite son, Absalom. Absalom was a really good-looking guy with great hair, a great personality, and tons of charisma. David was proud of him and David loved him. In every account we read of David and Absalom, David just couldn’t get enough of this kid. But Absalom was selfish, cunning, and ruthless. The men around David could see this, but he was blinded by his love. Here is a short little list of what Absalom did: Murdered his brother when his Dad did nothing to defend his sister's rape. Stole the heart of his dad’s people by sabotaging his reputation. Lied to his dad and declared himself king Took over the palace and the city. Had sex with 10 of David's women on the roof so the whole city could watch. Sent out a massive army to kill his Dad. And yet, David is still naïve and blind. His reply in the middle of this coup? “For my sake, deal gently with young Absalom.” Deal gently with him? Deal gently? This guy has taken your kingdom. This guy has turned the hearts of the people against you. This guy has disgraced you by having sex with your women on the roof of the city so everyone could see. Be gentle? David’s favoritism had blinded him. And it takes his captain Joab to confront him and help him see what a disaster his favoritism was to his people. The soldiers crept back in ashamed and embarrassed that they had saved David’s life and his kingdom. David was so obsessed over one son that he grieved an entire army that had fought for his life. His love made him lose perspective. His favoritism almost led to failure. You must guard against favoritism in your life. You must commit to growing into a man who treats people fairly. You must decide to do what is right and fair no matter what other people think. Romans 2:11 is clear about this. “For God does not show favoritism.” Look at how God talks about his hatred for favoritism and love for fairness in Deuteronomy 10:17-19: “For the LORD your God is the God of gods and Lord of lords. He is the great God, the mighty and awesome God, who shows no partiality and cannot be bribed. He ensures that orphans and widows receive justice. David should have surrendered his son to the Lord, rather than showering him with affirmation. He should have disciplined him with kindness, rather than giving him whatever he wanted. And yet David didn’t seem to learn. Trying to control people you love only pushes them away. Surrender them to the Lord, and do what’s right. This is a hard but essential lesson for a young man to learn. But if you learn it now, it will set you up for leadership and influence that honors God and helps others. We need men who treat others fairly in our world today, not those who show favoritism.
questions: Have you ever shown favoritism to someone and hurt someone else because of it? Would you be willing to share what happened?
Have you ever been overlooked by someone because they treated another as a favorite? What was it and how did it make you feel?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share some experiences of how damaging favoritism is in the adult world. How have they learned to deal with it when they have encountered it?

thoughts.verse: My dear brothers and sisters, how can you claim to have faith in our glorious Lord Jesus Christ if you favor some people over others? For example, suppose someone comes into your meeting dressed in fancy clothes and expensive jewelry, and another comes in who is poor and dressed in dirty clothes. If you give special attention and a good seat to the rich person, but you say to the poor one, “You can stand over there, or else sit on the floor”—well, doesn’t this discrimination show that your judgments are guided by evil motives? James 2:1-4 Read through his verse slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you a vision of being a young man who treats people fairly. Ask him for forgiveness if you have shown favoritism and comfort if someone has used favoritism against you.

days.title: Passive Parents
days.scripture: 1 KINGS 1:5-10; 1:11-39 About that time David’s son Adonijah, whose mother was Haggith, began boasting, “I will make myself king.” So he provided himself with chariots and charioteers and recruited fifty men to run in front of him. But his father had never once reprimanded him by saying, “Why do you act this way? ”Adonijah had been born next after Absalom, and he was very handsome. Adonijah took Joab son of Zeruiah and Abiathar the priest into his confidence, and they agreed to help him become king. But Zadok the priest, Benaiah son of Jehoiada, Nathan the prophet, Shimei, Rei, and David’s personal bodyguard refused to support Adonijah. Adonijah went to the Stone of Zoheleth near the spring of En-rogel, where he sacrificed sheep, cattle, and fattened calves. He invited all his brothers—the other sons of King David—and all the royal officials of Judah. But he did not invite Nathan the prophet or Benaiah or the king’s bodyguard or his brother Solomon. Then Nathan went to Bathsheba, Solomon’s mother, and asked her, “Haven’t you heard that Haggith’s son, Adonijah, has made himself king, and our lord David doesn’t even know about it? If you want to save your own life and the life of your son Solomon, follow my advice. Go at once to King David and say to him, ‘My lord the king, didn’t you make a vow and say to me, “Your son Solomon will surely be the next king and will sit on my throne”? Why then has Adonijah become king?’ And while you are still talking with him, I will come and confirm everything you have said.” So Bathsheba went into the king’s bedroom. (He was very old now, and Abishag was taking care of him.) Bathsheba bowed down before the king. “What can I do for you?” he asked her. She replied, “My lord, you made a vow before the LORD your God when you said to me, ‘Your son Solomon will surely be the next king and will sit on my throne.’ But instead, Adonijah has made himself king, and my lord the king does not even know about it. He has sacrificed many cattle, fattened calves, and sheep, and he has invited all the king’s sons to attend the celebration. He also invited Abiathar the priest and Joab, the commander of the army. But he did not invite your servant Solomon. And now, my lord the king, all Israel is waiting for you to announce who will become king after you. If you do not act, my son Solomon and I will be treated as criminals as soon as my lord the king has died.” …King David responded, “Call Bathsheba!” So she came back in and stood before the king. And the king repeated his vow: “As surely as the LORD lives, who has rescued me from every danger,your son Solomon will be the next king and will sit on my throne this very day, just as I vowed to you before the LORD, the God of Israel.” Then Bathsheba bowed down with her face to the ground before the king and exclaimed, “May my lord King David live forever!” Then King David ordered, “Call Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah son of Jehoiada.” When they came into the king’s presence, the king said to them, “Take Solomon and my officials down to Gihon Spring. Solomon is to ride on my own mule. There Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet are to anoint him king over Israel. Blow the ram’s horn and shout, ‘Long live King Solomon!’ Then escort him back here, and he will sit on my throne. He will succeed me as king, for I have appointed him to be ruler over Israel and Judah.” “Amen!” Benaiah son of Jehoiada replied. “May the LORD, the God of my lord the king, decree that it happen. And may the LORD be with Solomon as he has been with you, my lord the king, and may he make Solomon’s reign even greater than yours!” So Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, Benaiah son of Jehoiada, and the king’s bodyguard took Solomon down to Gihon Spring, with Solomon riding on King David’s own mule. There Zadok the priest took the flask of olive oil from the sacred tent and anointed Solomon with the oil. Then they sounded the ram’s horn and all the people shouted, “Long live King Solomon!” And all the people followed Solomon into Jerusalem, playing flutes and shouting for joy. The celebration was so joyous and noisy that the earth shook with the sound.
days.reflection: Passivity is the enemy of godly men. Passivity lets people take advantage of you. Passivity lets people take advantage of those you should defend. Passivity can destroy your life. As bad as this can be for your own life, one of the worst places that passivity shows up is in a dad's life. A passive dad will damage his kids. He will allow things into their life that destroys them, and he won’t take action when they are in danger. Sadly, in spite of all the good things David did, he was a passive dad. Did you see that highlighted verse in the reading today? Let’s look at it again for a second. “But his father had never once reprimanded him by saying, “Why do you act this way?” Adonijah was one of David’s sons. He was handsome. He was strong. He was confident. He also got to do whatever he wanted because David wasn’t involved in his life. And because Adonijah was used to getting his own way, he decided he wanted to be king. So he appointed himself king, hired people to go before him to celebrate his kingship, and tried to take the kingdom from his Father into his own hands. He didn’t check with his dad or discuss this with his brothers. Why? It says David “never once” tried to instruct his own son or question his behavior. Can you imagine that? Can you imagine your Dad never checking in on you to shape you and form you? Never saying no or helping you figure out how to live the right way? Proverbs 22:6 says this, “Direct your children onto the right path, and when they are older, they will not leave it.” David didn’t give Adonijah a path to follow or return to. David’s passivity meant his sons had no path into manhood. This meant that at the end of David’s life, his family was a disaster. Pursuing the heart of those we love must always overcome a tendency toward passivity. David didn’t even bother to ask the question, “Why do you act this way?” and as a result, he had no say in how his son ended up acting. I know you have discussed how important it is to reject passivity a few times so far as you have been going through the Primal Path, but now you can see even more clearly the damage it can do. David’s passivity destroyed his family. David’s passivity created chaos in his home. David’s passivity jeopardized his whole kingdom at the end of his life. Heed this warning loud and clear. Problems don’t go away if you ignore them. Problems don’t magically disappear if you stop thinking about them. Passivity makes problems worse. Passivity is the root of almost everything wrong in the world. At the beginning of the human story, Adam passively sat by while Satan deceived his wife Eve. He didn’t push back; he didn’t defend her; he didn’t take radical action. He just sat there, eating fruit like a dummy which he knew was wrong. His passivity led to disaster for the whole of the human family. Contrast that with Jesus. Jesus wasn’t passive. He resisted the devil, fought back against temptation, embraced the cross, and bought the kingdom of God to bear on the world. We experience life and salvation because of Jesus’ passion and proactivity. As you continue to grow into a man, be proactive and passionate like Jesus. Not passive and reactive like Adam. I can't help but imagine how different things would have been if David had parented differently. Surrender your passivity to God and embrace proactive passion instead. Then you will learn to handle life like a true and godly man.
questions: Do you think most guys your age lean toward being passive or proactive? Why?
What are some areas of your life where you can see some passive tendencies? How may they get you in trouble or cause problems later on?

Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a couple of examples from their own story of how passivity hurt them when they were your age and how being proactive helped them. Ask them for some advice on how to become more proactive.

thoughts.verse: My child, don’t reject the LORD’s discipline, and don’t be upset when he corrects you. For the LORD corrects those he loves, just as a father corrects a child in whom he delights. Proverbs 3:11-12 Read through his verse slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to help you in any areas you are struggling with passivity and to give you a vision of becoming a passionate and proactive man.

days.title: The Solomon Scramble
days.scripture: 1 KINGS 1:28-53 King David responded, “Call Bathsheba!” So she came back in and stood before the king. And the king repeated his vow: “As surely as the LORD lives, who has rescued me from every danger, your son Solomon will be the next king and will sit on my throne this very day, just as I vowed to you before the LORD, the God of Israel.” Then Bathsheba bowed down with her face to the ground before the king and exclaimed, “May my lord King David live forever!” Then King David ordered, “Call Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah son of Jehoiada.” When they came into the king’s presence, the king said to them, “Take Solomon and my officials down to Gihon Spring. Solomon is to ride on my own mule. There Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet are to anoint him king over Israel. Blow the ram’s horn and shout, ‘Long live King Solomon!’ Then escort him back here, and he will sit on my throne. He will succeed me as king, for I have appointed him to be ruler over Israel and Judah.” “Amen!” Benaiah son of Jehoiada replied. “May the LORD, the God of my lord the king, decree that it happen. And may the LORD be with Solomon as he has been with you, my lord the king, and may he make Solomon’s reign even greater than yours!” So Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, Benaiah son of Jehoiada, and the king’s bodyguard took Solomon down to Gihon Spring, with Solomon riding on King David’s own mule. There Zadok the priest took the flask of olive oil from the sacred tent and anointed Solomon with the oil. Then they sounded the ram’s horn and all the people shouted, “Long live King Solomon!” And all the people followed Solomon into Jerusalem, playing flutes and shouting for joy. The celebration was so joyous and noisy that the earth shook with the sound. Adonijah and his guests heard the celebrating and shouting just as they were finishing their banquet. When Joab heard the sound of the ram’s horn, he asked, “What’s going on? Why is the city in such an uproar?” And while he was still speaking, Jonathan son of Abiathar the priest arrived. “Come in,” Adonijah said to him, “for you are a good man. You must have good news.” “Not at all!” Jonathan replied. “Our lord King David has just declared Solomon king! The king sent him down to Gihon Spring with Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah son of Jehoiada, protected by the king’s bodyguard. They had him ride on the king’s own mule, and Zadok and Nathan have anointed him at Gihon Spring as the new king. They have just returned, and the whole city is celebrating and rejoicing. That’s what all the noise is about. What’s more, Solomon is now sitting on the royal throne as king. And all the royal officials have gone to King David and congratulated him, saying, ‘May your God make Solomon’s fame even greater than your own, and may Solomon’s reign be even greater than yours!’ Then the king bowed his head in worship as he lay in his bed, and he said, ‘Praise the LORD, the God of Israel, who today has chosen a successor to sit on my throne while I am still alive to see it.’” Then all of Adonijah’s guests jumped up in panic from the banquet table and quickly scattered. Adonijah was afraid of Solomon, so he rushed to the sacred tent and grabbed on to the horns of the altar. Word soon reached Solomon that Adonijah had seized the horns of the altar in fear, and that he was pleading, “Let King Solomon swear today that he will not kill me!” Solomon replied, “If he proves himself to be loyal, not a hair on his head will be touched. But if he makes trouble, he will die.” So King Solomon summoned Adonijah, and they brought him down from the altar. He came and bowed respectfully before King Solomon, who dismissed him, saying, “Go on home.”
days.reflection: There is an old saying that goes like this: “Failing to plan, is planning to fail.” In order for important things to move from one stage to another, there must be a plan. Imagine trying to go through high school and there was no order to your classes, no teachers assigned to help, and no required reading. That may sound like freedom, but it would be chaos. Who would determine the grades? Who would determine who passes? Who would determine if the work was done? People understand this, and that’s why education is planned out like it is. Or imagine a world-famous football coach sitting all the players down right before a championship game and saying, “You guys are really good, just go out there and do what you want. Pass the ball around and try and get some points on the board. Go for it.” Everyone would go crazy if a coach did that. Yet that is what King David did with his whole legacy. Even though God miraculously brought him from obscurity to power and gave him wealth and fame, he didn’t seem to think about what would happen next. He didn’t leave a clear plan for his family to navigate the challenges of running the kingdom after he was gone. The crazy behavior of his sons (sexual assault, murder, attempted overthrows, and betrayals) were the result of a failure to plan. So his family descended into power plays and total shambles because he failed to plan for the future. Right at the last minute, Bathsheba, Solomon's mom, comes in and asks David to anoint her son Solomon as king. He is willing to do this to keep his word, but it happens after 3 dead brothers, a divided army, and tension everywhere in the leadership of the nation. If David had laid out a clear plan and been willing to deal with the rivalry and fighting of his sons, things could have gone so differently. There could have been a stabilized and way less traumatized kingdom to leave to his son. Part of the challenge you will face as you grow up is learning to think strategically and skillfully about the future. Like a really good chess player, are going to have to learn to think several moves ahead. This may look like thinking through the long-term consequences of your actions instead of the short ones, planning what classes to take and how hard to work to get into the college you want, or deciding the kind of man you want to become to attract a godly wife. It will require intentionality and planning to work out. Making plans for the future may not sound really fun, but it can be. You can get a vision of what can happen next in such a way that it starts pulling you into what you want and away from childish things. It can help you to begin to prioritize what matters when others are just messing around. And it can help you say no to temptation and distraction because you have a clear vision of what you want. Ephesians 1 says that God planned out salvation way in advance and that he fulfilled that plan at just the right time. Your parents are also trying to help you plan your life so you can find your calling and purpose and live with joy. Boys scramble; men plan. A good plan helps everyone, scrambling last minute doesn’t. Learn the damage that can happen if you fail to plan from David, and resolve in your heart to learn to think ahead. Your life literally depends on it.
questions: With zero judgment, how would you rate yourself in terms of being good at planning from 1-10 (10 being the highest)?

Can you think of a favorite movie that involves the character working on a plan to make something happen? Or a part of a movie where a plan is hatched to rescue someone? Which one is it, why do you like it, and what can you learn about planning from it?

Ask the person you are doing this study with to share how they learned to value the importance of planning as a man. Ask them to share some stories from when they were your age about how “Failing to plan, is planning to fail."

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. Good planning and hard work lead to prosperity, but hasty shortcuts lead to poverty.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in any of them?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to help you become more strategic-minded and thoughtful about the future.

days.scripture: 2 SAMUEL 12:10-12 From this time on, your family will live by the sword because you have despised me by taking Uriah’s wife to be your own. “This is what the LORD says: Because of what you have done, I will cause your own household to rebel against you. I will give your wives to another man before your very eyes, and he will go to bed with them in public view. You did it secretly, but I will make this happen to you openly in the sight of all Israel.
days.reflection: Consequences, consequences, consequences. That’s what your actions have. Consequences. Some good, some bad. Everything you do has a cause and effect. Boys don’t think about consequences that much, but men do. Hopefully this week you have seen the incredible importance of how your decisions and actions can have ripple effects for generations to come. You may not think about this at all, but the decisions you are making right now will be a part of your story forever. When you look back and reflect on your teenage years, it’s the decisions you are making now that will tell the story, for good, for bad, for grace, or for rebellion. That’s why it’s so important to orient your heart toward God. David got so many things right in his life, and yet, this one area proved to be a real disaster. His lack of integrity with Bathsheba created chaos and havoc with his family. The guilt of the sin and adultery was forgiven by God, but the consequences bled out into his sons. David’s sons died of violent means, one of them was sexually immoral in ways that were worse than David, and another one was put to death by his brother Solomon. There are always long-range consequences for our actions. That’s why Galatians 6:7-8 is one of the most important verses you can remember as you grow into a man and one we will keep coming back to on The Primal Path. “Don’t be misled—you cannot mock the justice of God. You will always harvest what you plant. Those who live only to satisfy their own sinful nature will harvest decay and death from that sinful nature. But those who live to please the Spirit will harvest everlasting life from the Spirit. “ The world will try and tell you that you can do whatever you want without consequences. The world will try and tell you to rebel against God's word, God's standards, and God's people. But just take a look at human history. Rebellion leads to misery. Sin has a bitter taste. Prodigals will end up in the pig slop at one point or another. But grace can be powerful. Even though David sinned with Bathsheba, God redeemed the relationship. Her son Solomon was the one who succeeded David as the king and inherited the kingdom. Solomon was the one who would become the wisest man in the world. Solomon is the one who builds the temple for the presence of the Lord. Yet Solomon would fall into trouble with women as well, taking things further than David ever dreamed. What a complicated mess. Sin and grace and rebellion and redemption. Life is full of messy parts as we walk with God and fight against sin. That’s why it's important that you don’t just rush through life and do whatever you want. You have to think deeply about your actions, their consequences, and the legacy you want to leave. That’s why Haggai 1:7 says, “Give careful thought to your ways.” Don’t be passive and don’t be a control freak. Surrender things to God with your family and trust in him. That way there will be grace, blessing, and redemption that flow into other people’s lives because of you. You’re doing great so far. Keep going. You’ve got this!
questions: What stood out to you the most from this past week of devotions about King David’s messy family? Why?
What do you think King David would do differently with his family if he had his time again?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share about how their parents’ decisions shaped them. What are they grateful for and where is there tension? Why?

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. The godly walk with integrity; blessed are their children who follow them.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in any of them?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God for your family. Ask him to protect you and give your whole family grace to love and serve God. Pray for anyone in your family who may be having a tough time right now.

extras: WEEK 9 MANSCHOOL GENOGRAM MAN SCHOOL REMINDER "The recipe for fun is pretty simple when you’re raising boys: add to any activity an element of danger, stir in a little exploration, add a dash of destruction, and you’ve got yourself a winner." - John Eldredge OPTION 1. ACTIVITY: GENOGRAM Most guys have no real idea of their family story and history. They normally hear sanitized versions and snippets and don’t know why things have happened as they have. You have a chance to change that this week, and after seeing what happened with King David, your son may be especially open. There are a bunch of genograms available here you can download. You may also want to go back and look at the Genogram you did in the Primal Path PDF Field Guide that you got when you signed up. There are lots of really solid ways of thinking through things in there. https://tinyurl.com/56swkj22 Don’t make this a factual exercise; make this a story. Be as honest and honoring as you can. Share what is appropriate, but don’t hold back for the sake of being nice. Show the ways you have worked as a dad to break generational sin and release generational blessing, including by taking him through the Primal Path. Go as deep, wide, and long as his attention span lets you go. OPTION 2. WATCH THE PLACE BEYOND THE PINES Warning: this is a very harsh film with a ton of language. But I know of no other movies that tell the haunting drama of generational impact like this one. You may want to watch it yourself to see if you think your son is ready for it. It’s a loss of innocence a bit for sure, but no more than what King David did. Dad discernment is needed here, but this one will stay with you for the long haul. https://youtu.be/KepnbJT5nBw
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
